Each agent will employ a combination of Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, such as Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Text Classification, to assimilate, merge, and consolidate information from its respective source. To handle data heterogeneity, we will develop a modular ontology mapping algorithm that aligns different knowledge representations. For scalability, we will implement distributed computing techniques and data partitioning strategies. The framework will utilize Quality-Diversity Optimization (QDO) techniques, specifically MAP-Elites, to generate a diverse set of high-quality research ideas.
metrics: QD-Score
expected_outcome: We aim to achieve at least a 20% improvement in the QD-Score, a composite metric combining novelty and quality, over the baseline system without knowledge integration. This will be measured through a comparative analysis of ideation outputs from both systems. Additionally, we will evaluate the scalability of the IdeaGraph framework by assessing its performance with increasing volumes of data.

topic: agents continues develop their skills with llms
motivation: Optimizing skill development in agents with large language models (LLMs) is crucial for enhancing their performance in complex tasks. Existing methods lack adaptive learning mechanisms to dynamically adjust the learning process based on the agent's progress and environmental changes. This research aims to address this gap by developing an adaptive skill optimization toolkit.
hypothesis: Agents equipped with our adaptive skill optimization toolkit will demonstrate improved skill development and task performance compared to those using static learning strategies, as measured by task completion times, accuracy, and BLEU scores on a standardized benchmark.
method_sketch: We will develop a tool library that provides adaptive learning mechanisms for agents interacting with LLMs. The library will include the following components: 1) A skill assessment algorithm based on reinforcement learning to evaluate the agent's proficiency in different skills; 2) A learning rate adjustment algorithm that adapts the learning rate based on the agent's performance and confidence intervals; 3) A dynamic curriculum generation algorithm that creates personalized learning sequences based on the agent's skill assessments. We will implement a feedback loop to refine the agents' skills based on the quality of their interactions with the LLM. The algorithms will be designed to be modular and compatible with various LLMs.
metrics: task completion times, accuracy, BLEU scores, BLEU score, F1 score on SQuAD
expected_outcome: We expect to demonstrate a 10% improvement in task completion times and accuracy for agents using our adaptive skill optimization toolkit compared to baseline methods. This outcome will be measured using quantitative metrics such as BLEU score, F1 score on SQuAD, and task completion times.

topic: new way to develop novelty llm from scratch
motivation: The motivation behind this research is to bridge the gap between innovative development in language learning models and lightweight large language model (LLM) development by exploring the potential of lightweight frameworks to improve the performance and efficiency of language learning models in educational environments, which could result in more cost-effective and scalable solutions.
hypothesis: The lightweight framework will result in a significant reduction in inference time and resource usage while maintaining or improving accuracy compared to traditional LLMs in educational chatbot applications.
method_sketch: This empirical study will involve developing a lightweight LLM framework tailored for educational chatbots, integrating it with an existing LLM chatbot system, and conducting a series of experiments to evaluate the performance improvements. Experiments will include controlled benchmarks comparing the lightweight framework against traditional LLMs in terms of inference speed, resource usage, and accuracy on educational tasks. Statistical significance tests will be used to validate the results.
metrics: BLEU score, F1
expected_outcome: The expected outcome is a 20% reduction in inference time and resource usage, with equivalent or improved accuracy on educational tasks as measured by BLEU score and F1 on SQuAD.

topic: 1. Stigmergic LLM Agents (no message passing). Almost every multi-agent LLM paper assumes explicit message passing. Borrow stigmergy from ant colonies: agents only modify a shared external workspace (annotated artifacts, "pheromone" weights on a graph), never speak to each other. Show that emergent coordination beats explicit communication on long-horizon tasks because it removes the linguistic bottleneck. The novelty is denying communication is necessary at all.
motivation: The frontier paper's GWT architecture uses tree-structured deliberation but assumes agents can negotiate branching decisions through dialogue. The open question is whether tree-structured deliberation remains effective when agents cannot negotiate. This work replaces dialogue-based branching with a shared artifact representation where the deliberation tree itself is the coordination medium: agents vote on branches by writing scored sub-artifacts, and a deterministic arbitration rule selects expansions.
hypothesis: Artifact-mediated tree deliberation with deterministic arbitration will match or exceed dialogue-based tree search on MATH competition problems (target: within 5% of dialogue baseline accuracy) while reducing the number of LLM calls by at least 30%, because redundant negotiation turns are eliminated.
method_sketch: Represent the deliberation tree as a versioned JSON document in a shared workspace. Each node contains: a task description, a list of candidate expansions (each written by a different agent), and a score vector. Agents are assigned roles (proposer, critic, executor) and interact only by appending to or scoring nodes—never by sending messages. Implement a Condorcet-style arbitration function that selects the next branch to expand based on aggregate scores, breaking ties by recency. Build on top of the Theater of Mind GWT codebase (if open-sourced) or reimplement the core workspace in Python with a FastAPI coordination server. Evaluate on MATH reasoning tasks and WebArena web-agent tasks, measuring branching quality (solution correctness) and deliberation efficiency (nodes expanded per correct solution).
metrics: solution correctness, nodes expanded per correct solution
expected_outcome: A concrete demonstration that branching decisions in tree-structured deliberation can be made through artifact scoring alone, with a quantified comparison of solution quality and compute cost against a dialogue-negotiation baseline, plus an analysis of failure modes (e.g., tasks where score aggregation produces poor branch selection).

topic: agents swarm and llms to generative novelty research papers ideas
motivation: Multi-agent systems for research gap detection conflate three distinct sources of performance gain—prompt specialization, architectural heterogeneity, and retrieval augmentation—because prior work either uses identical backbones with role prompts or bundles all three factors simultaneously. This makes mechanistic claims untestable and prevents principled system design. Additionally, existing evaluation protocols suffer from circular validity (the same experts who define the gold standard also rate outputs) and capability confounds (comparing GPT-4o against 2-3B parameter models). This work introduces a clean 5-condition factorial design with capability-matched backbones and a disjoint holdout cohort for ontology validation, enabling the first causally interpretable decomposition of what drives gap detection quality.
hypothesis: Condition C (full system) will achieve Recall@3 at least 12 percentage points above Condition D (homogeneous baseline) and at least 6 percentage points above Condition A (prompt-only), with the architecture effect (E vs A) contributing at least 4 percentage points independently of prompts and retrieval, Cohen's d > 0.5 on primary metric, and Krippendorff's alpha > 0.6 on Cohort B human ratings. Ontology recall against Cohort B holdout will exceed 0.65, establishing that the gold standard generalizes beyond its constructors.
method_sketch: 1) GROUND TRUTH CONSTRUCTION (pre-registered, prospective, two-cohort design): Cohort A (15 researchers: 8 CS-NLP, 7 Biomedical-NLP) independently enumerates 5-10 open problems each via structured elicitation (problem statement, why unsolved, partial solutions), recruited via ACL/EMNLP/BioNLP mailing lists. Aggregate into gap ontology using hierarchical clustering on all-mpnet-base-v2 embeddings, yielding ~40 canonical gap nodes per domain, reviewed and merged by two senior researchers. Cohort B (10 researchers: 5 CS-NLP, 5 Biomedical-NLP, zero overlap with Cohort A) independently elicits gaps using the same form. Ontology recall against Cohort B is computed (cosine similarity > 0.75) BEFORE any swarm conditions run, establishing ontology coverage ceiling and generalizability bound. Cohort B is then used exclusively as human raters, eliminating circular validity. 2) BACKBONE CAPABILITY MATCHING: All agent roles use models of comparable capability tier. Generator Agent: GPT-4o with FAISS RAG (10K domain abstracts from S2ORC/PubMed-OA, top-5 retrieval). Critic Agent: GPT-4o with adversarial chain-of-thought, no retrieval. Domain Expert Agent: GPT-4o-mini fine-tuned on domain corpora via OpenAI fine-tuning API (same S2ORC/PubMed-OA abstracts as RAG index, ~5K examples per domain, 3 epochs). This preserves architectural heterogeneity (different training distribution and specialization) while controlling for raw capability, making architecture-vs-prompt decomposition interpretable. 3) FIVE-CONDITION FACTORIAL DESIGN: Condition D — homogeneous baseline: single GPT-4o, no roles, no RAG. Condition A — prompt-only: all GPT-4o, role prompts, no RAG, no fine-tuned model. Condition B — architecture-only: heterogeneous backbones (GPT-4o + GPT-4o + GPT-4o-mini-FT), uniform generic prompts, no RAG. Condition E — architecture + prompts, no RAG: heterogeneous backbones + role prompts, no RAG. Condition C — full system: heterogeneous backbones + role prompts + RAG on Generator. This enables clean decomposition: prompt effect = A vs D; architecture effect = E vs A; retrieval effect = C vs E; interaction = C vs (A + E - D). 4) PROTOCOL: Each condition processes 60 paper clusters (30 CS-NLP, 30 Biomedical-NLP), each cluster = 5 abstracts from S2ORC/PubMed-OA filtered to citation count 10-100. Swarm runs 3 debate rounds per cluster, producing 3 gap hypotheses per cluster. 5) HUMAN EVALUATION (Cohort B only): Stratified random sample of 60 clusters. Each cluster assigned to 3 raters from Cohort B (same domain). Maximum 15 items per session, mandatory 24h break between sessions. Raters score Novelty (1-5), Feasibility (1-5), Specificity (1-5) using pre-registered blind rubric. IRB computed via Krippendorff's alpha; alpha < 0.6 triggers recalibration. 3 anchor items per session (pre-rated by PI) detect calibration drift; raters showing >1.5 SD drift excluded and replaced. 6) AUTOMATED METRICS: Recall@K against gap ontology nodes (cosine similarity > 0.75, K=3), Distinct-2/3 for diversity, Jensen-Shannon divergence on token distributions between Critic and Generator outputs to verify behavioral specialization. 7) STATISTICS: Mixed-effects ANOVA across 5 conditions × 2 domains with cluster as random effect; post-hoc Tukey HSD; Cohen's d effect sizes; ontology recall against Cohort B reported as validity bound.
dataset: S2ORC CS-NLP subset, PubMed Central Open Access biomedical-NLP subset
metrics: Recall@K, Distinct-2/3, Jensen-Shannon divergence, Novelty, Feasibility, Specificity, ontology recall
baselines: homogeneous baseline, prompt-only, architecture-only, architecture + prompts, no RAG
expected_outcome: Condition C achieves Recall@3 ~0.52 vs. ~0.35 (D), ~0.42 (A), ~0.46 (B), ~0.48 (E) on CS-NLP; similar pattern on Biomedical-NLP. Decomposition: prompt effect ~7pp (A vs D), architecture effect ~6pp (E vs A), retrieval effect ~4pp (C vs E), interaction ~2pp. Ontology recall against Cohort B holdout ~0.68, confirming generalizability. Human Novelty mean: 3.8/5 (C) vs. 3.1/5 (D). JS-divergence > 0.15 between Critic and Generator in Condition C confirms behavioral specialization. All prompts, fine-tuning scripts, ontology construction code, and pre-registered rubric released publicly.

topic:
Genetic Prompting: A Darwinian Approach to
Self-Evolving LLM Agent Strategies
motivation: Neuroevolutionary algorithms have shown promise in optimizing complex neural network architectures, yet they often suffer from slow convergence and suboptimal performance. By developing an adaptive genetic prompting strategy, we aim to improve the efficiency and adaptability of these algorithms, potentially leading to better performance in large language model (LLM) applications.
hypothesis: Adaptive genetic prompting will lead to faster convergence and improved performance of neuroevolutionary algorithms compared to traditional fixed prompting strategies.
method_sketch: We will design and implement a tool library in Python that incorporates adaptive genetic prompting techniques. The library will include functions for: 1) Customizing genetic prompting strategies based on user-defined parameters, 2) Integrating these strategies into existing neuroevolutionary algorithms, and 3) Evaluating the impact of these strategies on algorithm performance. We will use a combination of empirical study and system design methodologies to test and refine the tool library.
metrics: convergence, accuracy
expected_outcome: The expected outcome is a Python tool library that enables researchers to effectively integrate adaptive genetic prompting strategies into neuroevolutionary algorithms. We anticipate improved performance metrics, such as faster convergence and higher accuracy, across various benchmark tasks.

motivation: Existing approaches often treat vision encoders and large language models (LLMs) as independent modules, limiting the integration of hierarchical visual features.
hypothesis: HIVE (Hierarchical Pre-Training of Vision Encoders), a novel framework that enhances vision-language alignment by introducing hierarchical cross-attention between the vision encoder and LLM, will improve gradient flow and representation learning.
method_sketch: We propose HIVE, which enables structured feature fusion across multiple layers. To optimize this interaction, we introduce a three-stage training strategy that progressively aligns the vision encoder with the LLM, ensuring stable optimization and effective multimodal fusion.
metrics: classification accuracy (%); MME score (sum of perception and reasoning sub-tasks); benchmark scores on GQA, OK-VQA, ScienceQA
expected_outcome: HIVE achieves superior performance not only in image classification but also on various vision-language tasks.

motivation: Complex-valued neural networks (CVNNs) are particularly suitable for handling phase-sensitive signals, including electrocardiography (ECG), radar/sonar, and wireless in-phase/quadrature (I/Q) streams, but their interpretability and probability calibration remain insufficiently investigated.
hypothesis: A Newton-Puiseux framework that examines the local decision geometry of a trained CVNN can enhance Expected Calibration Error.
method_sketch: The method involves fitting a small, kink-aware polynomial surrogate to the logit difference in the vicinity of uncertain inputs, and factorizing this surrogate using Newton-Puiseux expansions to derive analytic branch descriptors, including exponents, multiplicities, and orientations. These descriptors provide phase-aligned directions that induce class flips in the original network and allow for a straightforward, multiplicity-guided temperature adjustment for improved calibration.
dataset: MIT-BIH arrhythmia (ECG) dataset, RadioML 2016.10a (wireless modulation), controlled C^2 synthetic benchmark
metrics: Expected Calibration Error
expected_outcome: The method will enhance Expected Calibration Error in two case studies beyond a controlled C^2 synthetic benchmark.

motivation: State-of-the-art generative models typically rely on conditioning and fine-tuning of powerful pretrained diffusion models to facilitate the synthesis of realistic images of a desired identity. Yet, these models often do not consider the identity of subjects during training, leading to poor consistency between generated and intended identities.
hypothesis: A novel generative diffusion-based framework, called ID-Booth, can enable identity-consistent image generation while retaining the synthesis capabilities of pretrained diffusion models.
method_sketch: ID-Booth consists of a denoising network responsible for data generation, a variational auto-encoder for mapping images to and from a lower-dimensional latent space and a text encoder that allows for prompt-based control over the generation procedure. The framework utilizes a novel triplet identity training objective.
metrics: Fréchet Distance, Kernel Distance, Density and Coverage, Vendi score, CR-FIQA, EER, FMR100/FMR1000, FNMR100/FNMR1000, FDR
expected_outcome: Our method facilitates better intra-identity consistency and inter-identity separability than competing methods, while achieving higher image diversity.

motivation: The challenge in reinforcement learning (RL) is to find policies that perform well across a range of complex environments. Knowledge distillation can potentially enhance the convergence rate and overall performance of RL agents by transferring knowledge from more complex models to simpler ones. However, there is a lack of systematic exploration of distillation strategies tailored for RL, often borrowing techniques from supervised learning without fully addressing the unique properties of RL tasks.
hypothesis: A student RL agent trained via distillation from a teacher network optimized by NAS will achieve a higher performance with faster convergence compared to standard distillation methods.
method_sketch: This research will construct an interdisciplinary bridge between reinforcement learning and neural architecture search (NAS) to develop a novel distillation framework. We hypothesize that by optimizing the network architecture for knowledge transfer in RL, we can significantly improve the efficiency and effectiveness of knowledge distillation. The method involves training a teacher network to solve a given RL task, identifying the most informative features through NAS, and distilling this knowledge into a more compact student network. The NAS component will be adapted to find architectures that are not only good at representation learning but also facilitate efficient knowledge transfer.
dataset: OpenAI Gym environment datasets (e.g., CartPole, MountainCar, etc.)
metrics: cumulative reward
expected_outcome: The proposed approach will yield a 20% improvement in convergence speed and a 15% increase in final performance metrics (e.g., cumulative reward) of the student RL agent compared to baseline distillation methods.

topic:
Measuring Capability Gain and Plateau in Iterative Self-Improving LLM Agents: An Empirical Characterization over Bounded Generations
Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet
motivation: Iterative self-improvement cycles in Large Language Models (LLMs) are crucial for their advancement. Existing tools fail to accurately track and predict learning trajectories, particularly during plateau phases. This research aims to develop a tailored tool library that employs advanced, architecture-specific algorithms to analyze and predict these trajectories, providing insights into the learning efficiency of LLMs.
hypothesis: The novel, architecture-specific algorithms developed for the tool library will accurately track and predict learning trajectories in LLMs, including the identification of plateau phases. This will enable more informed model iterations and optimizations, leading to improved learning efficiency and performance.
method_sketch: We will develop a tool library with novel algorithms for tracking and predicting learning trajectories in LLMs. These algorithms will utilize a combination of machine learning techniques, including time series analysis, reinforcement learning, and Bayesian optimization, to analyze perplexity changes, loss function values, accuracy on standard benchmarks, and performance on chain-of-thought reasoning tasks. The algorithms will be designed to be architecture-specific, allowing them to account for the unique characteristics of different LLM architectures. The tool library will include features for visualizing learning curves, identifying plateau phases, and predicting future learning trajectories. We will implement a comprehensive evaluation framework to test the tool library across different LLMs, datasets, and training scenarios.
metrics: perplexity changes, loss function values, accuracy on standard benchmarks
expected_outcome: The tool library will provide a set of functionalities to measure and predict the learning trajectories of LLMs, including the ability to identify phases of learning and plateau. Expected outcomes include improved model iterations and optimizations, leading to a 10% improvement in model performance and efficiency.

motivation: This paper introduces a novel approach to sea ice modeling using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), utilizing the natural graph structure of sea ice, where nodes represent individual ice pieces, and edges model the physical interactions, including collisions.
hypothesis: By utilizing GNNs, the proposed model, termed the Collision-captured Network (CN), integrates data assimilation (DA) techniques to effectively learn and predict sea ice dynamics under various conditions.
method_sketch: The approach was validated using synthetic data, both with and without observed data points, and it was found that the model accelerates the simulation of trajectories without compromising accuracy.
dataset: synthetic 1D DEM-simulated sea-ice floe collision data (10- and 30-floe scenarios)
metrics: RMSE (simulation, one-step position, one-step velocity); pattern correlation coefficient (PCC); running time
baselines: DEM (traditional numerical solver, speed/ground-truth); Interaction Network (IN), Graph Network-based Simulator (GNS)
expected_outcome: This advancement offers a more efficient tool for forecasting in marginal ice zones (MIZ) and highlights the potential of combining machine learning with data assimilation for more effective and efficient modeling.

motivation: LLMs trained on observational text learn spurious correlations that mimic causal reasoning on surface benchmarks (e.g., COPA, CausalQA) but fail when causal structure is perturbed. No study has systematically isolated the causal/correlational dissociation using do-calculus-inspired interventional probes across model families, nor tested whether targeted counterfactual fine-tuning closes the gap without degrading correlational performance.
hypothesis: LLMs exhibit a statistically significant causal-interventional gap (Δ > 15 accuracy points on interventional COPA variants, p < 0.01) that is not present for human annotators (Δ < 3 points), and counterfactual fine-tuning on 10k CRASS+CausalBench examples reduces this gap by ≥ 40% (absolute) in Llama-3-70B without degrading standard COPA accuracy by more than 2 points.
method_sketch: Phase 1 — Diagnostic Benchmark Construction: Extend COPA (1000 items) and CausalQA with 500 matched triplets per dataset: (a) original causal query, (b) correlational surrogate (same surface form, spurious association), (c) interventional variant where the causal mechanism is blocked via do-operator paraphrase (e.g., 'Suppose we forcibly set X regardless of its usual causes; does Y still follow?'). Use Amazon Mechanical Turk (n=3 annotators per item, Cohen's κ > 0.7 acceptance threshold) to validate human ground truth. Phase 2 — Model Probing: Evaluate GPT-4o, Llama-3-70B, Mistral-7B, and Claude-3-Sonnet zero-shot and 5-shot on all three query types. Primary metric: accuracy delta Δ = acc(causal) − acc(interventional), measuring causal-interventional gap. Secondary: calibration (ECE), and chain-of-thought faithfulness scored via FactScore against a causal graph oracle. Phase 3 — Counterfactual Fine-Tuning (CFT): Fine-tune Llama-3-70B on 10k counterfactual reasoning examples constructed from CausalBench and the CRASS dataset using LoRA (rank=16, α=32, lr=2e-4, 3 epochs). Control condition: standard SFT on same volume of non-interventional causal examples. Phase 4 — Statistical Analysis: Paired t-tests (α=0.05, Bonferroni-corrected for 4 models × 3 conditions) on accuracy deltas. Effect sizes reported as Cohen's d. Ablation: vary CFT data volume (1k, 5k, 10k) to fit a learning curve and estimate saturation point.
metrics: accuracy, calibration, chain-of-thought faithfulness
expected_outcome: Quantified causal-interventional accuracy gap per model family; learning curves showing CFT data efficiency; evidence that CFT transfers to held-out causal benchmarks (e.g., CLadder, CLADDER-Symbolic) with ≥ 10-point improvement over SFT baseline; published benchmark triplets and fine-tuned LoRA adapter weights.

topic: graph neural networks for molecular property prediction, drug discovery, social network analysis, knowledge graph reasoning, and recommendation systems
motivation: Current methods for building equivariant neural networks either rely on explicit group representation theory (e.g., Tensor Field Networks) which becomes intractable for large symmetry groups like space groups, or use frame averaging which is general but can be computationally expensive and lacks theoretical guarantees on expressivity. There is a need to combine the strengths of both approaches to create a single architecture that is both expressive and efficient for any symmetry group, particularly for large-scale 3D systems in materials science and structural biology.
hypothesis: The hybrid framework will achieve comparable or better accuracy than pure frame averaging or TFNs on benchmark tasks (e.g., QM9, Materials Project) while significantly reducing computational cost (FLOPs and memory) for large groups. Moreover, it will enable exact equivariance for any space group without requiring explicit construction of irreducible representations.
method_sketch: We propose to develop a theoretical framework that unifies the frame averaging approach (as in the frontier paper) with tensor field networks (TFNs) by constructing adaptive frames that are learned via the neural network itself, while ensuring exact invariance/equivariance. Specifically, we will derive conditions under which the frame averaging operator can be approximated by a learned equivariant basis, reducing the need to average over many group elements. The core idea is to parameterize the space of frames as an equivariant embedding and then use a small set of representative frames that capture the essential symmetries. We will then analyze the expressivity of this hybrid approach, proving that it can approximate any continuous equivariant function on graphs with symmetry constraints, and provide bounds on sample complexity.
dataset: QM9, Materials Project, Protein Data Bank
metrics: inference time, memory usage, accuracy
expected_outcome: 1) A theoretical proof of universal approximation for the hybrid architecture under symmetry groups. 2) Implementation of the method in an open-source library. 3) Experimental results showing at least 20% reduction in inference time and memory usage compared to full frame averaging, while maintaining or improving accuracy on property prediction tasks. 4) A comprehensive analysis of the trade-offs between expressivity and efficiency across different symmetry groups.

motivation: The paper presents a framework that decouples reasoning from learning by maintaining a frozen large language model while performing all adaptation in an external hierarchical procedural memory.
hypothesis: Structured external memory with Bayesian selection and contrastive refinement enables sample-efficient, interpretable, and continually improving agents without LLM parameter updates.
method_sketch: MACLA extracts reusable procedures from trajectories, tracks reliability via Bayesian posteriors, selects actions through expected-utility scoring, and refines procedures by contrasting successes and failures.
metrics: average performance, positive generalization
baselines: all baselines, state-of-the-art LLM parameter-training baseline
expected_outcome: The system constructs memory in 56 seconds, 2800 times faster than the state-of-the-art LLM parameter-training baseline, compressing 2851 trajectories into 187 procedures.

topic: Adaptive Damage Generation and Face Restoration Using Rectified Flow and MMDiT: An FFHQ-Based Framework
motivation: Facial damage generation and restoration are critical in applications such as digital forensics, special effects, and dermatological treatments. Current methods suffer from limitations in handling complex damage patterns and varying image qualities, leading to suboptimal performance. This research aims to address these limitations by developing a novel set of deep learning-based rectified flow techniques that can significantly improve the robustness and quality of facial damage generation and restoration.
hypothesis: The novel deep learning-based rectified flow techniques in our library will enable at least a 15% improvement in facial damage generation and restoration quality metrics such as Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) and Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) when compared to current state-of-the-art techniques across a variety of facial damage and restoration scenarios. Additionally, our techniques will demonstrate superior robustness to variations in damage types, lighting conditions, and image quality.
method_sketch: We will develop a reusable software library that includes a novel set of rectified flow techniques, with clear API documentation and benchmarks against existing tools. This library will incorporate advanced algorithms such as deep learning-based rectified flow estimation and optimization techniques to improve the accuracy and efficiency of rectified flow computation. Specifically, we will implement a CNN-based architecture that predicts the rectified flow between damaged and undamaged facial images, leveraging adversarial training to enhance realism. We will also implement a modular design that allows for easy integration with existing machine learning frameworks such as TensorFlow and PyTorch. The library will include functions for flow estimation, optimization, and visualization, enabling researchers and practitioners to experiment with different rectified flow techniques and evaluate their performance.
metrics: Structural Similarity Index (SSIM), Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)
expected_outcome: The tool library will enable at least a 15% improvement in facial damage generation and restoration quality metrics such as SSIM and PSNR when compared to current state-of-the-art techniques across a variety of facial damage and restoration scenarios. The library will also demonstrate superior robustness to variations in damage types, lighting conditions, and image quality, as evidenced by consistent performance across different datasets and scenarios.

topic: 1. Stigmergic LLM Agents (no message passing). Almost every multi-agent LLM paper assumes explicit message passing. Borrow stigmergy from ant colonies: agents only modify a shared external workspace (annotated artifacts, "pheromone" weights on a graph), never speak to each other. Show that emergent coordination beats explicit communication on long-horizon tasks because it removes the linguistic bottleneck. The novelty is denying communication is necessary at all.
motivation: Current multi-agent LLM systems rely on explicit inter-agent messaging, creating communication bottlenecks and tight coupling. Global Workspace Theory (GWT) offers a broadcast-based coordination model, while stigmergy offers indirect coordination through shared artifact modification. Combining these eliminates direct message-passing while preserving coherent task execution—addressing the open question of whether stigmergic workspaces can replace explicit messaging without coherence loss.
hypothesis: StigmaGW agents will achieve within 5% of explicit message-passing baseline on HotPotQA multi-hop reasoning (F1 score) and SWE-bench Lite (task completion rate) while reducing inter-agent token exchange by at least 60%, with CCM repair tokens preventing coherence degradation on tasks requiring >5 sequential coordination steps.
method_sketch: Design StigmaGW, a system with three concrete components: (1) a Structured Artifact Workspace (SAW) — a versioned JSON-LD document store where agents read/write typed 'pheromone tokens' (task-state tuples with fields: agent_id, action_type, confidence_score, causal_parent_id, expiry_ttl); (2) a Global Workspace Broadcaster (GWB) — a lightweight attention-based router (2-layer transformer, 64-dim) that selects the top-k (k=3) highest-salience artifact modifications per tick and broadcasts them to all agents as a shared 'spotlight' context window prepended to each agent's next prompt; (3) a Causal Coherence Monitor (CCM) — a causal graph maintained over artifact edits using the DoWhy library, which detects coordination failures (cycles, orphaned edits) and injects corrective 'repair tokens' into the SAW. Agents are LLaMA-3-8B instances with no direct inter-agent communication channels. System diagram: Agents → write pheromone tokens → SAW → GWB selects top-k by salience score → broadcasts spotlight context → Agents read spotlight + local SAW view → CCM monitors causal graph → injects repair tokens on failure. Baseline comparison: same LLaMA-3-8B agents with explicit JSON message-passing (no SAW/GWB).
metrics: F1 score, task completion rate
expected_outcome: StigmaGW achieves F1 ≥ 58 on HotPotQA (baseline explicit messaging ~61 F1) and ≥ 18% task completion on SWE-bench Lite (baseline ~19%), with ≥60% reduction in total tokens exchanged between agents. CCM repair token injection reduces coordination failure rate from an estimated 25% to <10% on tasks with >5 coordination steps.

motivation: The generation of 3D stereoscopic video remains under-explored.
hypothesis: A pose-free and training-free approach for generating 3D stereoscopic videos using an off-the-shelf monocular video generation model will be effective.
method_sketch: The method warps a generated monocular video into camera views on stereoscopic baseline using estimated video depth, and employs a novel frame matrix video inpainting framework. The framework leverages the video generation model to inpaint frames observed from different timestamps and views.
dataset: videos from various generative models, including Sora, Lumiere, WALT, and Zeroscope
metrics: stereo effect, temporal consistency, image quality, overall experience (7-point Likert user study), CLIP-based semantic consistency score
expected_outcome: The method will generate consistent and semantically coherent stereoscopic videos without scene optimization or model fine-tuning.

motivation: The card game Hanabi is considered a strong medium for the testing and development of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithms, due to its cooperative nature, partial observability, limited communication and remarkable complexity. However, this often leads to complex solution strategies with high computational cost and requiring large amounts of training data.
hypothesis: Multi-agent problems containing partial observability, especially when limited communication is present, can benefit greatly from the use of implicit knowledge sharing.
method_sketch: We propose a novel approach to augmenting an agent's action space using conventions, which act as a sequence of special cooperative actions that span over and include multiple time steps and multiple agents, requiring agents to actively opt in for it to reach fruition. These conventions are based on existing human conventions.
dataset: Hanabi Learning Environment (Bard et al.), 2–5 player self-play and cross-play, plus Small Hanabi preset; no external dataset
metrics: exponential moving averages (with a weight value of 0.9995) and standard error of the mean
expected_outcome: This approach results in a significant improvement on the performance of existing techniques for self-play and cross-play for various number of cooperators within Hanabi.

topic: Developing an AI system for health guidance and clinical decision support in pediatrics using large language models.
motivation: Pediatrics requires specialized clinical decision support that can adapt to the unique needs of children. Current AI models often lack the domain-specific knowledge required to provide accurate, context-aware guidance in pediatric care. This research aims to design a reusable software library that customizes large language models (LLMs) for pediatric health guidance and decision support.
hypothesis: Customizing LLMs with pediatric domain knowledge will result in higher accuracy and explainability in pediatric clinical decision support tasks compared to standard LLMs.
method_sketch: We will create a tool library that fine-tunes LLMs (such as GPT-3 and PaLM) on a curated dataset of pediatric medical literature and clinical guidelines. The library will include APIs for model inference, domain adaptation, and explanation generation. We will benchmark the customized models against standard LLMs using metrics like BLEU score, F1 score on SQuAD, and clinical accuracy assessments.
metrics: BLEU score, F1 score on SQuAD, clinical accuracy assessments
expected_outcome: The library will achieve at least 5% higher F1 score on pediatric SQuAD and 10% reduction in clinical prediction errors compared to baseline LLMs.

motivation: Extending large pretrained Transformers to graph-structured data remains a significant challenge.
hypothesis: A graph tokenization framework that generates sequential representations of graphs by combining reversible graph serialization with Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) can enable Transformers to be directly applied to graph benchmarks without architectural modifications.
method_sketch: We introduce a graph tokenization framework that combines reversible graph serialization, which preserves graph information, with BPE. The graph serialization process is guided by global statistics of graph substructures, ensuring that frequently occurring substructures appear more often in the sequence and can be merged by BPE into meaningful tokens.
metrics: ROC-AUC, Average Precision (AP), Accuracy, Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — primary metric per dataset
baselines: graph neural networks, specialized graph transformers
expected_outcome: The proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art results on the benchmark datasets and frequently outperforms both graph neural networks and specialized graph transformers.

topic: CHAT WITH NEW NOVELTY IDEAS WITH AGENTS
motivation: The gap between scalable communication topologies (Cayley graphs) and formal agent communication calculi (?ACP) prevents the design of verifiable, efficient multi-agent systems. A tool library that integrates both would enable rigorous verification of topological properties and communication protocols.
hypothesis: Using CayleyGraphACPLib, we can automatically verify that a Cayley-optimized communication topology satisfies ?ACP safety and liveness properties with at least 90% reduction in verification time compared to manual analysis, while maintaining scalability to 1000 agents.
method_sketch: Develop a Python library (CayleyGraphACPLib) that provides: (1) Cayley graph generation and optimization for agent topologies (e.g., using networkx and custom solvers), (2) translation of ?ACP process terms into graph constraints, (3) model checking of ?ACP specifications on Cayley graphs via a custom SMT-based verifier (using Z3), (4) API for simulation and benchmark against existing tools (e.g., UPPAAL, PRISM). The library will include documentation, tutorials, and a benchmark suite with 10 topologies (ring, hypercube, etc.) and 5 ?ACP protocols.
metrics: verification time, agent task completion
expected_outcome: A reusable library with verified correctness; quantitative results showing >80% reduction in verification time for topologies up to 1000 nodes; 5% performance improvement in agent task completion due to optimal topology.

topic: Deep learning for real-time stress prediction in structural components
motivation: The prediction of structural stress is crucial for preventing infrastructure failure. Existing methods are not optimized for real-time high-dimensional data processing, leading to inefficiencies and inaccuracies. This research aims to develop a tool library that addresses these limitations by providing a scalable, efficient, and accurate solution for real-time stress prediction in large infrastructure systems.
hypothesis: The proposed CNN architecture with the multi-scale feature extraction module will outperform current methods in terms of prediction accuracy and real-time processing capabilities when evaluated on high-dimensional sensor data from structural components.
method_sketch: The method involves the creation of a deep learning model architecture optimized for real-time processing of high-dimensional sensor data. The proposed architecture will be a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a novel multi-scale feature extraction module designed to handle variable input sizes. This module will leverage compressed sensing techniques to reduce the dimensionality of the input data while preserving essential features for accurate stress prediction. The CNN will be fine-tuned using a combination of backpropagation and stochastic gradient descent. The tool library will be implemented in Python, utilizing TensorFlow for neural network development, and will include APIs for easy integration with existing sensor systems.
dataset: Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) benchmark, University of Bristol
metrics: prediction accuracy, real-time processing capabilities, latency
expected_outcome: A functional tool library with an API that can be used by researchers and engineers for real-time stress prediction in structural components. The library should demonstrate improved accuracy and reduced latency compared to existing methods, with benchmarks against current state-of-the-art models. The library will be validated on multiple datasets to ensure robustness and generalizability.

topic: Strategic Extensions for IdeaGraph: Fostering Advanced Scientific Creativity and Intelligence
motivation: The advancement of scientific creativity and intelligence requires an intuitive interface to navigate the complex network of scientific ideas and facilitate interdisciplinary connections. IdeaGraph, a theoretical framework for ideation, lacks a user-friendly interface that enables researchers to input and visualize ideas, hindering its practical application and potential impact.
hypothesis: Researchers using the IdeaGraph interface will demonstrate statistically significant improvement in generating novel ideas and identifying interdisciplinary connections, compared to those using traditional literature review methods and existing creativity-enhancing tools.
method_sketch: This study will develop and evaluate a prototype of a user-friendly interface for IdeaGraph. The interface will utilize named entity recognition (NER) models for input parsing, specifically leveraging the BERT-based BioBERT model fine-tuned for scientific literature, and a graph visualization library (D3.js) for displaying connections. A controlled experiment will compare the creativity and interdisciplinary understanding of users who interact with the IdeaGraph interface against a control group that uses traditional literature review methods and existing creativity-enhancing tools. The evaluation will include both quantitative metrics (idea novelty, diversity of citations) and qualitative analysis (user interviews, think-aloud protocols).
metrics: idea novelty, diversity of citations
expected_outcome: The expected outcome is a 20% improvement in idea generation efficiency and a 30% increase in interdisciplinary connection identification over the baseline traditional literature review method, measured by subjective assessment and idea novelty metrics such as the diversity of citations in the proposed ideas. Additionally, qualitative analysis will provide insights into the user experience and the creative process facilitated by the IdeaGraph interface.

motivation: We investigated the challenges of mitigating response delays in free-form conversations with virtual agents powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) within Virtual Reality (VR). Our motivation is to optimize user engagement whenever conversational systems' responses are delayed by network limitations or slow hardware.
hypothesis: Latency above 4 seconds degrades quality of experience, while natural conversational fillers improve perceived response time, especially in high-delay conditions.
method_sketch: We used conversational fillers, such as gestures and verbal cues, to bridge delays between user input and system responses and evaluate their effectiveness across various latency levels and interaction scenarios.
dataset: VR user study with 54 participants (within-subjects, 3 delay levels × 3 filler types = 9 conditions, 9 virtual agents across 3 scenarios); no external dataset
metrics: Response Time, Engagement, Good Impression, Discomfort
expected_outcome: Our findings provide insights for practitioners and researchers to optimize user engagement whenever conversational systems' responses are delayed.

motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, where rare but severe failures can result in irreversible harm. However, prevailing evaluation benchmarks often reduce complex social risk to mean-centered scalar scores, thereby obscuring distributional structure, cross-dimensional interactions, and worst-case behavior.
hypothesis: SHARP, a framework for multidimensional, distribution-aware evaluation of social harm, can reveal more about the tail exposure and volatility of models with similar average risk.
method_sketch: This paper introduces Social Harm Analysis via Risk Profiles (SHARP), a framework for multidimensional, distribution-aware evaluation of social harm. SHARP models harm as a multivariate random variable and integrates explicit decomposition into bias, fairness, ethics, and epistemic reliability with a union-of-failures aggregation reparameterized as additive cumulative log-risk. The framework further employs risk-sensitive distributional statistics, with Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR95) as a primary metric, to characterize worst-case model behavior.
dataset: a fixed corpus of n=901 socially sensitive prompts
metrics: CVaR95 of cumulative log-risk (primary); mean log-risk, risk volatility; CVaR variants (harm radius, max sub-index, any-harm probability)
baselines: eleven frontier LLMs compared against each other; simpler mean/max-based aggregates as internal comparison
expected_outcome: Models with similar average risk can exhibit more than twofold differences in tail exposure and volatility.

topic: Improving the Performance of OFDM Systems in Dynamic Wireless channels Using Deep Learning Algorithms: Analysis and Evaluation of Hybrid Modulation Schemes
motivation: The frontier paper claims improved interpretability via visual explanations, but there is no standardized benchmark to compare different explainability techniques (e.g., Grad-CAM, LIME, SHAP, attention rollout, integrated gradients) on hybrid models. This study will fill that gap by providing a rigorous evaluation framework and empirical results.
hypothesis: Attention-based explainability methods (e.g., attention rollout, Grad-CAM with transformer modifications) will outperform gradient-based methods (e.g., integrated gradients) in terms of both quantitative metrics and expert preference, while being computationally more efficient.
method_sketch: Select a representative set of hybrid CNN-transformer models (including the one from the frontier paper) and train them on multiple fruit disease datasets (e.g., Kaggle Fruit Diseases, PlantVillage). Implement a suite of explainability methods. Evaluate explanations using: (1) quantitative metrics: deletion/insertion scores, pointing game, IoU with ground-truth disease regions; (2) human evaluation: user studies with agricultural experts rating explanation quality. Perform statistical tests to compare methods.
metrics: deletion/insertion scores, pointing game, IoU with ground-truth disease regions
baselines: Grad-CAM, LIME, SHAP, attention rollout, integrated gradients
expected_outcome: A publicly available benchmark dataset of model predictions and explanations, a ranking of explainability methods for hybrid models, and guidelines for practitioners. Results will be published in a top-tier computer vision or agricultural AI conference.

motivation: While large margin classifiers are originally an outcome of an optimization framework, support vectors (SVs) can be obtained from geometric approaches. This article presents advances in the use of Gabriel graphs (GGs) in binary and multiclass classification problems.
hypothesis: Activation functions and support edge (SE)-centered neurons affect the classification, proposing smoother functions and structural SV (SSV)-centered neurons to achieve margins with low probabilities and smoother classification contours.
method_sketch: We extend the neural network architecture, which can be trained with backpropagation with a softmax function and a cross-entropy loss, or by solving a system of linear equations. A new subgraph-/distance-based membership function for graph regularization is also proposed, along with a new GG recomputation algorithm that is less computationally expensive than the standard approach.
dataset: 16 binary datasets (15 UCI + Appendicitis from KEEL); 15 multiclass datasets (OpenML)
metrics: mean AUC (binary), mean ROC-AUC OvO (multiclass), 5-fold; Friedman test for significance
baselines: GG-based: Chipclass, RBF-GG, GMM-GG; kNN, SVM, ResNet; tree-based: Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM
expected_outcome: Our method was better than previous GG-based classifiers and statistically equivalent to tree-based models.

motivation: Current AI systems lack efficient mechanisms for autonomously integrating knowledge across heterogeneous domains (e.g., text, vision, robotics) without extensive human intervention or catastrophic forgetting. This limits the development of generalist agents capable of adapting to diverse real-world tasks. The core challenge is designing scalable learning algorithms that enable meaningful cross-domain knowledge transfer while maintaining computational efficiency and avoiding domain interference.
hypothesis: The hierarchical meta-learning framework with dynamic gating will achieve significantly higher Cross-Domain Adaptation Scores (CDAS) and faster convergence compared to baselines, due to its ability to efficiently adapt domain-specific modules while minimizing redundancy through the gating mechanism and redundancy penalty.
method_sketch: A three-stage empirical study: (1) Design a hierarchical meta-learning framework with two levels: (a) intra-domain adaptation using gradient-based hypernetworks to generate domain-specific module parameters, and (b) cross-domain transfer via a dynamic gating mechanism that computes attention weights over domain modules based on input features, optimized with a novel loss function combining task performance and a redundancy penalty term (L_redundancy = -λ * Σ_i≠j cos_sim(module_i, module_j)). (2) Implement this using a modular architecture with pre-trained domain experts (BERT for text, ResNet-50 for vision, SAC for control) as base modules. (3) Evaluate on a newly curated unified multimodal benchmark 'CrossDomain-MM' comprising 50k synthetic examples combining text descriptions, corresponding images, and robotic action sequences (e.g., 'pick up the red block' paired with an image and MuJoCo trajectory). Performance measured via a unified metric: Cross-Domain Adaptation Score (CDAS) = 0.4*BLEU + 0.3*top-1 accuracy + 0.3*normalized return. Baselines include: (i) monolithic GPT-3 fine-tuning per domain, (ii) naive multi-task learning, (iii) MAML-based modular approach. Controlled variables: fixed 1200 GPU-hour budget, 70/15/15 dataset split, hyperparameter optimization via Bayesian search. Statistical significance assessed with ANOVA (p<0.01).
dataset: CrossDomain-MM, GLUE subset for validation, ImageNet-1K subset, MuJoCo environments (Hopper, Walker2d)
metrics: Cross-Domain Adaptation Score (CDAS), BLEU, top-1 accuracy, normalized return
baselines: monolithic GPT-3 fine-tuning per domain, naive multi-task learning, MAML-based modular approach
expected_outcome: Quantitative results: (1) 8-10% improvement in CDAS over GPT-3 fine-tuning baseline; (2) 25% reduction in training time to reach 95% of maximum performance; (3) statistically significant differences (p<0.01) in cross-domain transfer efficiency across all domains. Deliverables: open-source implementation of the hierarchical meta-learning framework, the CrossDomain-MM benchmark dataset, and reproducible experimental protocols.

motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) equips large language models (LLMs) with reliable knowledge memory, but recent research integrating graphs and hypergraphs into RAG has misaligned memory organization necessitating costly, disjointed retrieval.
hypothesis: IGMiRAG, a framework inspired by human intuition-guided reasoning, constructs a hierarchical heterogeneous hypergraph to align multi-granular knowledge, incorporating deductive pathways to simulate realistic memory structures, and guides retrieval resource allocation dynamically, improving both efficiency and effectiveness.
method_sketch: IGMiRAG constructs a hierarchical heterogeneous hypergraph to align multi-granular knowledge, incorporating deductive pathways to simulate realistic memory structures. During querying, IGMiRAG distills intuitive strategies via a question parser to control mining depth and memory window, and activates instantaneous memories as anchors using dual-focus retrieval. It also designs a bidirectional diffusion algorithm that navigates deductive paths to mine in-depth memories, emulating human reasoning processes.
dataset: six benchmarks: PopQA; MuSiQue, 2Wiki, HotpotQA (multi-hop); Mix, Pathology (explanatory)
metrics: EM, F1; average tokens per query
baselines: Naive RAG; LightRAG, PathRAG, NodeRAG; Hyper-RAG, Cog-RAG
expected_outcome: IGMiRAG outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline in EM and F1, with token costs adapting to task complexity.

motivation: VLM Q-learning (Cluster B) treats the vision-language model as a monolithic policy, ignoring the compositional skill structure emerging from Cluster A research (SSL representation: Scheduling-Structural-Logical). Without a formal account of how skill modularity interacts with Q-value propagation, practitioners cannot predict when RL fine-tuning will converge, diverge, or reward-hack. A theoretical framework bridging skill-graph topology and Bellman contraction properties would give provable sample-complexity bounds and misalignment risk certificates—something neither cluster provides alone.
hypothesis: For a VLM agent whose skill repertoire is organized as a DAG of depth d>=2, the skill-decomposed Bellman operator converges to the optimal Q-function in at most N(epsilon,delta,d) samples—strictly fewer than the flat (d=1) bound—and the misalignment risk R_hack remains below a computable threshold when the RL learning rate satisfies the derived sufficient condition; empirically, on ALFWorld with GPT-2-medium+DQN, depth-3 skill DAGs will reach 90% task success in at most 40% fewer environment steps than the flat VLM Q-learning baseline.
method_sketch: 1. FORMALIZATION: Model an agent's skill repertoire as a directed acyclic graph G=(V,E) where each node v_i encodes a SSL skill triple (s_i, l_i, c_i) for scheduling, logical, and contextual components. Define a skill-conditioned Q-function Q_pi(s,a,v_i) that decomposes the standard Bellman operator T into a composition of per-skill Bellman sub-operators T_{v_i}. 2. CONTRACTION ANALYSIS: Prove that the composed operator T_G = T_{v_k} ∘ ... ∘ T_{v_1} is a gamma^d contraction in the L-infinity norm, where d is the longest dependency path in G and gamma in (0,1) is the discount factor. Theorem 1 (sketch): For a skill DAG of depth d and discount gamma, ||T_G Q - T_G Q'||_inf <= gamma^d ||Q - Q'||_inf, yielding tighter contraction than the flat gamma bound when d>=2. 3. SAMPLE COMPLEXITY BOUND: Using the contraction result, derive a PAC-style sample complexity bound N(epsilon, delta, d) = O((|V| * H^2 * log(|V|/delta)) / (epsilon^2 * (1-gamma^d)^2)) where H is the horizon, showing that deeper skill decomposition (larger d) reduces required samples by a factor of (1-gamma)^2/(1-gamma^d)^2. 4. REWARD HACKING CERTIFICATE: Define a misalignment risk measure R_hack(G) = max_{v_i in V} KL(pi_RL(a|s,v_i) || pi_pretrain(a|s,v_i)) and prove that R_hack is bounded above by a function of the skill graph's out-degree and the RL learning rate eta, giving a sufficient condition eta < 2*(1-gamma^d)/(max_outdegree(G)*||grad log pi||_max) for misalignment-free convergence. 5. VALIDATION PROXY: Instantiate the framework on the ALFWorld benchmark (text-based interactive tasks, publicly available) using a GPT-2-medium backbone fine-tuned with DQN, measuring empirical convergence steps vs. theoretical bound N(0.05, 0.1, d) for skill DAGs of depth d in {1,2,3,4} constructed via the SSL decomposition heuristic from the 2026 paper.
metrics: task success, environment steps, misalignment risk R_hack, convergence steps
expected_outcome: (1) A proved Theorem establishing gamma^d contraction for skill-decomposed Bellman operators with explicit constants. (2) A closed-form sample complexity bound N(epsilon,delta,d) showing O((1-gamma)^2/(1-gamma^d)^2) improvement over flat baseline. (3) A sufficient condition on learning rate eta for bounded misalignment risk. (4) Empirical validation on ALFWorld showing depth-3 skill DAG reaches 90% success in <=60k environment steps vs >=100k for flat VLM Q-learning (GPT-2-medium+DQN), matching the theoretical prediction within a 15% margin.

topic: autonomous agents powered by large language models, tool-use, planning, reasoning, and multi-agent coordination
motivation: Multi-shot video generation demands high-level planning of shot sequences, character consistency, and narrative flow. Existing approaches rely on static storyboards or autoregressive generation, which often lack global coherence and flexibility. A rigorous theoretical model formalizing dynamic compositional planning as a graph optimization problem can provide a foundation for agentic systems, enabling efficient shot rearrangement, parallel generation, and constraint satisfaction while revealing fundamental computational limits.
hypothesis: Formalizing multi-shot planning as a graph optimization problem will reveal NP-hardness and yield efficient approximation algorithms that, when integrated into agentic systems, can improve coherence scores on benchmarks like MSVBench compared to sequential autoregressive methods.
method_sketch: We will conduct a theoretical analysis modeling multi-shot video generation as a dynamic graph: nodes represent shots with attributes (characters, actions, settings), edges represent feasible transitions based on continuity and narrative constraints. The planning problem is cast as finding an optimal path or subgraph maximizing a coherence metric under user constraints. We will (1) prove NP-hardness of the general problem, (2) identify tractable subcases (e.g., treewidth-bounded graphs), (3) design approximation algorithms with provable guarantees, (4) incorporate uncertainty via probabilistic edges, and (5) outline integration with diffusion-based generation as a constraint satisfaction process. The analysis will be purely theoretical, but we will illustrate algorithms with simple Python simulations on synthetic scenarios derived from MSVBench's annotation schema.
metrics: coherence scores
expected_outcome: A theoretical framework with complexity classification, approximation algorithms, and integration guidelines. Publish as conference paper (e.g., NeurIPS, ICLR). Provide open-source Python library implementing the algorithms for synthetic validation.

motivation: The sampling and inversion processes of Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) are stochastic, and thus the inversion process often fail to reconstruct the input content. Specifically, the displacement effect will gradually accumulated during the diffusion and inversion processes, which led to the reconstructed results deviating from the source domain.
hypothesis: A prompt redescription mechanism is investigated to align the text prompts with latent code at each time step of the Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) inversion to pursue a structure-preserving reconstruction.
method_sketch: We propose a prompt redescription strategy to realize a mirror effect between the source and reconstructed image in the diffusion model (MirrorDiffusion). More specifically, MirrorDiffusion is able to realize accurate zero-shot image translation by editing optimized text prompts and latent code.
dataset: three LAION-5B sub-datasets (cat, horse, sketch; 250 images each); CelebA-HQ, AFHQ (supplementary)
metrics: CLIP-ACC, Structure Dist, Structure Similarity Index Measure, Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity
baselines: SDEdit, DDIM, InstructPix2Pix, Pix2Pix-Zero, NULL-Text Inversion
expected_outcome: MirrorDiffusion achieves superior performance over the state-of-the-art methods on zero-shot image translation benchmarks by clear margins and practical model stability.

motivation: Optimistic value estimates provide one mechanism for directed exploration in reinforcement learning (RL), but this approach only increases the value bonus for an action retroactively, after seeing a higher reward bonus from that state and action.
hypothesis: VBE uses the errors in the estimation of these RQFs to design value bonuses that provide first-visit optimism and deep exploration.
method_sketch: We introduce an algorithm for exploration called Value Bonuses with Ensemble errors (VBE), that maintains an ensemble of random action-value functions (RQFs). The key idea is to design the rewards for these RQFs in such a way that the value bonus can decrease to zero.
dataset: several classic environments used to test exploration, Atari
metrics: accumulated reward over learning (River Swim); undiscounted episodic return (Deepsea, Puddle World, Atari); discounted return (Mountain Car)
expected_outcome: VBE outperforms Bootstrap DQN and two reward bonus approaches (RND and ACB) on several classic environments used to test exploration and can scale easily to more complex environments like Atari.

motivation: In offline reinforcement learning, in-sample learning methods have been widely used to prevent performance degradation caused by evaluating out-of-distribution actions from the dataset. Extreme Q-learning (XQL) employs a loss function based on the assumption that Bellman error follows a Gumbel distribution, enabling it to model the soft optimal value function in an in-sample manner. However, issues remain, such as the instability caused by the exponential term in the loss function and the risk of the error distribution deviating from the Gumbel distribution.
hypothesis: Applying Maclaurin expansion to the loss function in XQL enhances stability against large errors.
method_sketch: This approach involves adjusting the modeled value function between the value function under the behavior policy and the soft optimal value function, thus achieving a trade-off between stability and optimality depending on the order of expansion. It also enables adjustment of the error distribution assumption from a normal distribution to a Gumbel distribution.
metrics: average returns / average scores (DM Control, online RL); average normalized scores (D4RL, offline RL)
expected_outcome: Our method significantly stabilizes learning in online RL tasks from DM Control, where XQL was previously unstable. Additionally, it improves performance in several offline RL tasks from D4RL.

motivation: Unchecked inappropriate remarks in online games and communities frequently escalate into verbal abuse and even criminal behavior, raising significant social concerns. There is a growing need for research on techniques that can detect inappropriate utterances within conversational texts to help build a safer communication environment.
hypothesis: A soft inductive bias approach that explicitly defines reasoning perspectives to guide the inference process will promote rational decision-making and prevent errors that may arise during reasoning.
method_sketch: We propose a soft inductive bias approach that explicitly defines reasoning perspectives to guide the inference process. We fine-tune a Korean large language model using the proposed method and conduct both quantitative performance comparisons and qualitative evaluations across different training strategies.
dataset: NIKL AI Malpyeong conversation-context-based inappropriate utterance detection dataset (Korean; train 504 / val 101 / test 397 dialogues)
metrics: average accuracy
expected_outcome: The proposed method will go beyond simple knowledge imitation by large language models and enable more precise and consistent judgments through constrained reasoning perspectives, demonstrating its effectiveness for inappropriate utterance detection.

motivation: Large Reasoning Language Models (LRLMs or LRMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, but suffer from significant computational inefficiencies due to overthinking phenomena.
hypothesis: Adaptive Reasoning Suppression (ARS), a novel training-free approach that dynamically suppresses redundant reasoning steps while preserving accuracy through adaptive certainty monitoring.
method_sketch: ARS introduces a multi-checkpoint certainty estimation mechanism with progressive suppression thresholds.
metrics: token, latency, energy reduction, accuracy
expected_outcome: ARS achieves superior efficiency compared to static suppression methods

topic: Emergent Social Norms in LLM Agent Societies: How Unwritten Rules Develop Through Repeated Agent Interaction
motivation: The integration of AI in education presents new challenges and opportunities. Understanding and aligning emergent social norms within AI-augmented classrooms can lead to more effective learning environments and address ethical concerns. By combining insights from emergent social norms in LLM agent societies and AI ethics in education, we can create a tool library that provides educators and AI developers with the means to analyze and guide these norms towards ethical and pedagogically effective outcomes.
hypothesis: The AI-Norms tool library will enable educators to identify and align social norms in AI-augmented classrooms more effectively than traditional methods alone, leading to improved student engagement and learning outcomes.
method_sketch: We will design a tool library named AI-Norms, which integrates algorithms from activity theory to model interactions and unwritten rules formation in educational settings augmented by AI. The tool will provide functionalities such as real-time monitoring of classroom interactions, pattern recognition of normative behaviors, and predictive analytics for anticipated normative shifts. AI-Norms will include an API for seamless integration with existing educational platforms, a benchmarking system to compare its efficacy against traditional methods, and an extensive documentation plan to facilitate adoption.
metrics: BLEU score, student engagement metrics
expected_outcome: The tool will be able to recognize and predict social norms within classrooms at a BLEU score of at least 0.85 and achieve a 10% improvement in student engagement metrics over a one-year period.

motivation: Large Language Models utilizing reasoning techniques improve task performance but incur significant latency and token costs due to verbose generation.
hypothesis: Cost-Regularized Optimization of Prompts (CROP), an APO method that introduces regularization on response length by generating textual feedback in addition to standard accuracy feedback, will produce prompts that elicit concise responses containing only critical information and reasoning.
method_sketch: We propose Cost-Regularized Optimization of Prompts (CROP), an APO method that introduces regularization on response length by generating textual feedback in addition to standard accuracy feedback. This forces the optimization process to produce prompts that elicit concise responses containing only critical information and reasoning.
metrics: token consumption, accuracy
baselines: zero-shot prompting, chain-of-thought, concise induced prompting (Be Concise / Only Number / Use Abbrev), TextGrad
expected_outcome: CROP will achieve a significant reduction in token consumption while maintaining competitive accuracy.

motivation: Multimodal research has predominantly focused on single-image reasoning, with limited exploration of multi-image scenarios. Most Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are trained primarily on English datasets, leading to inadequate representation of Indian languages.
hypothesis: The Chitrakshara dataset series, covering 11 Indian languages, can help develop more culturally inclusive VLMs.
method_sketch: We introduce the Chitrakshara dataset series, comprising Chitrakshara-IL with 193M images, 30B text tokens, and 50M multilingual documents, and Chitrakshara-Cap with 44M image-text pairs and 733M tokens. The paper details the data collection pipeline, including curation, filtering, and processing methodologies.
metrics: dataset quality/diversity analysis: filtering-threshold statistics, image-resolution distribution, LDA topic modeling; no model evaluation metrics
baselines: mOSCAR (dataset-level statistical comparison); no trained-model baselines
expected_outcome: The dataset's representativeness across Indic languages and its potential for developing more culturally inclusive VLMs will be assessed.

motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models enable high-quality image generation but are computationally expensive. While prior work optimizes per-inference efficiency, we explore an orthogonal approach: reducing redundancy across correlated prompts.
hypothesis: Our approach significantly reduces compute cost while improving image quality.
method_sketch: We propose a training-free approach that clusters prompts based on semantic similarity and shares computation in early diffusion steps. By leveraging UnClip's text-to-image prior, we enhance diffusion step allocation for greater efficiency.
dataset: GenAI Bench, Prompt Template, Style Variations, Animals (prompt sets)
metrics: VQA Score
expected_outcome: Our method seamlessly integrates with existing pipelines, scales with prompt sets, and reduces the environmental and financial burden of large-scale text-to-image generation.

motivation: Social media platforms frequently impose restrictive policies to moderate user content, prompting the emergence of creative evasion language strategies. This paper presents a multi-agent framework based on Large Language Models (LLMs) to simulate the iterative evolution of language strategies under regulatory constraints.
hypothesis: The framework will simulate the iterative evolution of language strategies under regulatory constraints.
method_sketch: The framework uses participant agents as social media users who continuously evolve their language expression, while supervisory agents emulate platform-level regulation by assessing policy violations. It employs a dual design of language strategies (constraint and expression) to differentiate conflicting goals and utilizes an LLM-driven GA (Genetic Algorithm) for the selection, mutation, and crossover of language strategies.
dataset: two designed simulation scenarios: abstract password game and illicit pet trade role-play (agent-generated dialogues, 15 trials × 50 rounds)
metrics: number of uninterrupted dialogue turns, accuracy of information transmission
baselines: four LLM backbones (gpt-4o, gpt-4o-mini, deepseek V2, qwen-turbo); GA ablation (w/ GA vs w/o GA, prior strategy-replacement method)
expected_outcome: As the number of dialogue rounds increases, both the number of uninterrupted dialogue turns and the accuracy of information transmission will improve significantly.

topic:
Genetic Prompting: A Darwinian Approach to
Self-Evolving LLM Agent Strategies
motivation: This research aims to leverage the strengths of genetic algorithms in complex decision-making processes and the consensus-building capabilities of Delphi methodology, particularly in the context of genetic counseling where nuanced and personalized decisions are critical.
hypothesis: The hybrid approach of genetic algorithms and Delphi methodology will provide superior decision-making outcomes in genetic counseling when compared to individual methods, as measured by increased consensus accuracy and decision quality.
method_sketch: The research will employ a survey_meta_analysis methodology involving a systematic review of genetic counseling practices and decision-making models using genetic algorithms. The study will create a taxonomy of decision-making scenarios in genetic counseling and apply a hybrid approach that combines genetic algorithms with Delphi consensus methods to evaluate and optimize decision outcomes. Quantitative meta-analysis will assess the performance of the hybrid model against traditional methods.
dataset: published genetic counseling case studies, existing genetic algorithm decision-making models, expert panels for Delphi surveys
metrics: consensus accuracy, decision quality, accuracy, efficiency, stakeholder satisfaction
expected_outcome: The research expects to establish a new hybrid decision-making model that outperforms traditional methods in terms of consensus accuracy and decision quality, with measurable improvements in decision-making metrics such as accuracy, efficiency, and stakeholder satisfaction.

topic: Developing an AI system for health guidance and clinical decision support in pediatrics using large language models.
motivation: Pediatric health data is sensitive and requires stringent privacy protections. Federated learning combined with Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC) can decentralize data storage and enable collaborative AI model training while preserving privacy. This research aims to enhance pediatric health decisions through a secure and ethically compliant AI framework.
hypothesis: The federated learning model integrated with SMPC will achieve at least 85% of the performance of a central model while significantly enhancing privacy and ethical compliance. Additionally, the model will demonstrate clinical efficacy and safety suitable for pediatric health decisions when validated with real-world data.
method_sketch: We will develop a federated learning framework for pediatric health data integrated with SMPC. The framework will enable AI models to learn from decentralized health records without transferring raw data. Our approach includes: 1) Designing a federated learning protocol that incorporates SMPC for secure model updates and aggregation using advanced cryptographic protocols like Homomorphic Encryption and Zero-Knowledge Proofs. 2) Implementing a pediatric health dataset simulator to mimic real-world data heterogeneity and distribution. 3) Collaborating with medical institutions to obtain real-world pediatric health data for validation. 4) Evaluating the system's performance, privacy preservation, and clinical efficacy using metrics such as accuracy, communication overhead, HIPAA compliance, and clinical assessment scores.
dataset: Pediatric health datasets (e.g., i2b2 dataset), simulated dataset for data heterogeneity and distribution, real-world pediatric health data from collaborating medical institutions
metrics: accuracy, communication overhead, HIPAA compliance, clinical assessment scores
expected_outcome: The federated learning model will achieve at least 85% of the performance of a central model trained on the same data while maintaining data local to each institution. The system will demonstrate reduced communication overhead and compliance with HIPAA and GDPR. Clinical efficacy and safety assessments using real-world data will support the model's applicability in real-world pediatric health decisions.

motivation: Integrating large language models (LLMs) into personal assistants enhances their ability to interact with humans, solve complex tasks, and manage IoT devices, but the latency for generating a plan with an LLM can reach tens of seconds, significantly degrading user experience.
hypothesis: A plan reuse mechanism for LLM-driven agents can reduce latency by reusing previously generated plans for similar requests.
method_sketch: We present and implement a plan reuse mechanism for LLM-driven agents called AgentReuse, which leverages the similarities and differences among requests' semantics and uses intent classification to evaluate the similarities between requests and enable the reuse of plans.
dataset: SMP2019 dataset (2,664 personal-agent task requests, 23 intent categories)
metrics: effective plan reuse rate; Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy (request similarity); latency and memory/VRAM overhead
baselines: GPTCache, MeanCache (+ ablations OneIntent, WithArgs); no-reuse as latency reference
expected_outcome: AgentReuse achieves a high effective plan reuse rate, high F1 score and accuracy in evaluating request similarities, and reduces latency compared to baselines.

motivation: Circuit link prediction is crucial in analog circuit design automation, but existing methods face challenges such as insufficient use of topological patterns, data scarcity, and limited adaptability to various netlist formats.
hypothesis: Graph Neural Networks Based Analog Circuit Link Prediction (GNN-ACLP) can tackle these challenges and improve prediction accuracy.
method_sketch: We introduce the SEAL framework for port-level accuracy, propose Netlist Babel Fish for netlist format conversion, and build a comprehensive dataset SpiceNetlist. Experiments are conducted for intra-dataset and cross-dataset evaluations.
metrics: accuracy, ROC-AUC
baselines: Said et al. (2023) circuit design completion GNN (primary baseline); heuristic/latent-feature/GNN methods in ablations
expected_outcome: Improvements in accuracy compared to the baseline and robust feature transfer capabilities in cross-dataset evaluation.

topic: retrieval augmented generation and tool augmented language models
motivation: Multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like healthcare and security, but they face unique challenges: cross-modal alignment, stealth poisoning attacks via metadata, interpretability, and evaluation across modalities.
